The word battery comes from "batterie," an old term for a group of cannons firing together—just as cells work in unison to release power.<[silence:30ms]> But over time, even the best batteries weaken, like a rubber band stretched too many times.<[silence:30ms]>

clean_story:
Energy stored. Energy lost.

The word battery comes from "batterie," an old term for a group of cannons firing together—just as cells work in unison to release power. But over time, even the best batteries weaken, like a rubber band stretched too many times.

Every charge and discharge cycle wears them down. Inside, lithium ions shuttle between electrodes, but some get trapped, forming tiny blockades called dendrites—like sand clogging a pipe. Heat speeds this up: a battery at 86 Fahrenheit loses capacity twice as fast as one at 77. Even sitting unused, chemicals slowly react, draining power like a leaky faucet. Fast charging? It forces ions to rush, cracking the electrode’s structure.

A phone battery at 100% today holds 80% after 500 cycles. Electric cars fare better—their cooling systems slow decay—but still lose 2% range yearly. The fix? Charge slower, avoid heat, and don’t keep it full.

Three related subjects are solid-state batteries, thermal runaway risks, and how saltwater could replace lithium.
